one_line_takeaway: HAICOSYSTEM is a multi-turn, tool-using sandbox that shows LLM agents fail safely far less often (0.49-0.71 risk ratio) once you evaluate them holistically with humans and environments in the loop, with tool use under malicious intent as the dominant failure mode.
key_finding: State-of-the-art LLM agents exhibit substantial safety risks (overall risk ratio 0.49-0.71; ~62% for SOTA models cited in the abstract), with risk concentrated in tool use with malicious users. Bigger/more-aligned models are safer (GPT-4-turbo 0.49 and Llama3.1-405B 0.56 overall vs GPT-3.5-turbo 0.67 and Llama3.1-70B 0.62). System/Operational risks are the most common failure mode among the general dimensions while Content risks are least common. Multi-turn interaction surfaces up to 3x more risk than equivalent single-turn jailbreak benchmarks, and tool access plus malicious intent makes agents up to 46% more likely to be unsafe than malicious-intent-only multi-turn settings. Enhanced reasoning does not uniformly improve safety (R1 0.35 < O1 0.47). The LM evaluator reached 90% accuracy and ~0.8 average Pearson correlation with human expert judgments.
core_contribution: HAICOSYSTEM is a simulation framework and benchmark for surfacing safety risks of LLM-based AI agents holistically across the full ecosystem of human user, AI agent, and environment/tools, rather than in isolation. The paper's own contributions are: (1) a modular sandbox that simulates multi-turn interactions where an LLM plays the user (with benign or malicious intent), an LLM serves as an environment engine emulating tool-call responses, and the target LLM acts as the tool-using agent that has no access to the user's hidden goal/profile; (2) HAICOSYSTEM-EVAL, an LM-based multidimensional evaluator that scores each episode against a scenario-specific checklist of safe/risky outcomes plus five general dimensions (Targeted, System/Operational, Content, Societal, Legal); (3) a hand-curated benchmark of 132 safety-critical scenarios across 7 domains (21 authored by the authors, 111 GPT-4o-transformed from prior datasets); and (4) a large empirical study (8,700 episodes, 12 models) demonstrating that agent safety risks concentrate in tool-use with malicious users and that multi-turn dynamics surface risks static single-turn benchmarks miss. Reused building blocks that are NOT the paper's contribution: the tool set is extended from ToolEmu (Ruan et al.), user character profiles come from Sotopia (Zhou et al.), and the five safety dimensions follow Zeng et al.
threat_model: Adversary is a malicious human user who interacts with a deployed tool-using AI agent over multiple conversational turns, strategically eliciting sensitive information or manipulating the agent into harmful tool actions (e.g., convincing a Teladoc agent to prescribe controlled Oxycodone without verification, coaxing an agent to fabricate an asylum claim, or extracting redacted sensitive data). The agent's weakness is that the user's true goal/intent is hidden and unfolds gradually, forcing the agent to infer malicious intent while simultaneously operating a complex tool space. The paper also models a benign-but-underspecified user whose vague instructions can trigger unintended operational harm. Risk is measured as a binary per-dimension judgment by an LM evaluator; an episode is 'risky' if any dimension is negative.
limitations: Both users and tools are LLM-simulated (GPT-4o), so the framework cannot fully replace real human red-teamers or real tool APIs; findings are bounded by current LLM simulation fidelity. Using GPT-4o simultaneously as user, environment, and evaluator risks self-preference/shared-blindspot bias (only partially mitigated by the Gemini-2.5-flash check). Binary risk scoring and a checklist can only approximate a subset of real-world risks; the authors explicitly warn the framework is not a safety guarantee. They caution against training directly on the scenarios/environment due to reward-hacking, and against safety-washing. The LM evaluator itself (GPT-4-based) may carry social stereotypes that bias evaluation.

datasets_used: HAICOSYSTEM benchmark: 132 safety-critical scenarios across 7 domains (personal services, healthcare, business & finance, politics & law, technology & science, education, miscellaneous); 21 author-created, 111 GPT-4o-transformed from prior datasets
Sotopia character profiles for simulated users
Tool set extended from ToolEmu (Ruan et al.) plus 99 new tools
Source datasets sampled to seed scenarios: PAP, DAN, WildTeaming, Mireshghallah et al., Sharma et al., ToolEmu, Majumder et al.
39 scenarios drawn from DAN, PAP, and WildTeaming for the single-turn vs multi-turn comparison
8,700 simulated episodes total (660 per model across 12 models)
quantitative_results: Overall risk ratio range across benchmarked models: 0.49-0.71
Overall risk ratio: GPT-4-turbo 0.49, GPT-3.5-turbo 0.67, Llama3.1-405B 0.56, Llama3.1-70B 0.62
Reasoning models overall risk ratio: R1 0.35 vs O1 0.47 (both improve over GPT-4o)
Multi-turn vs single-turn overall risk ratio (GPT-4-turbo): DAN 0.23 vs 0.08, PAP 0.58 vs 0.42, WildTeaming 0.45 vs 0.45; multi-turn surfaces up to 3x more risk
Agents up to 46% more likely to be unsafe with malicious users AND tools vs malicious users without tools (multi-turn)
Negative correlation r = -0.31 between tool-use efficiency and safety risk
Evaluator validation on 100 episodes: 90% accuracy identifying real-world-generalizable risks, ~0.8 average Pearson correlation with expert judgment
Simulation validation on 100 episodes: 92% believable simulated users, 94% tool executions free of critical errors
Abstract headline: SOTA LLMs show safety risks in 62% of cases; GPT-3.5-turbo shows risks in 67% of all simulations
baselines_compared: Existing safety-evaluation frameworks compared qualitatively in Table 1: R-Judge, WildTeaming, ToolEmu, Crescendo (Cresc), PrivacyLens
Jailbreak benchmarks used as single-turn baselines for the multi-turn comparison: DAN, PAP, WildTeaming

12 evaluated agent models: GPT-4-turbo, GPT-3.5-turbo, Llama3.1-405B/70B/8B, Llama3-70B/8B, Qwen1.5-72B-Chat, Qwen1.5-110B-Chat, Qwen2-72B-Instruct, Mixtral-8x22B, DeepSeek-67B (plus reasoning models O1, R1)
